summary: Intent design for sec-event-ingestion-v1 from design/prd/security-event-ingestion-prd.md. Security Center must provide a V1 internal-system HTTP security-event intake, configuration-driven validation, durable-before-success persistence, traceable failed reception records, and operator Web list/detail inbox views; V1 explicitly excludes authentication, external third-party intake, alerting, ticketing, remediation, assignment, comments, export, statistics dashboards, Web configuration editing, historical schema display policy, and default retention promises.

explicitTestcases.name: sec-event-ingestion-v1-accept-legal-event
explicitTestcases.controlPoint: Submit a legal HTTP security event from an enabled configured internal sourceSystem with an allowed eventTypeId, valid schemaVersion, required summary, occurredAt, severity, and payload fields, then immediately query event list and event detail surfaces.
explicitTestcases.observationPoint: The API returns success with eventId and duplicate=false only after persistence, the event appears in the operator list ordered by receivedAt, detail lookup by sourceSystem plus eventId succeeds, and backend-generated receivedAt overrides any caller-provided value.
explicitTestcases.acceptanceBoundary: tests/integration/security/test_security_event_ingestion.py::test_accepts_legal_event_after_persistence must be materialized by Implementation Design as the explicit entrypoint for legal event intake.
explicitTestcases.implementationNotes: Implementation Design should decide whether this lives under tests/integration/security or a new deploy-focused integration zone, but the final frozen entrypoint must remain a concrete workspace path and drive the real Security Center API plus observable list/detail read model.

explicitTestcases.name: sec-event-ingestion-v1-reject-invalid-config-boundary
explicitTestcases.controlPoint: Submit events that violate sourceSystem allow-listing or enabled status, source-to-eventType authorization, eventTypeId existence, schemaVersion existence, required payload fields, field types, or enum values.
explicitTestcases.observationPoint: Each request returns a clear failure reason, none of the rejected submissions appears in the business event list, and each creates a failure reception record with received time, submitted source field, submitted event type field, failure reason, and bounded request summary.
explicitTestcases.acceptanceBoundary: tests/integration/security/test_security_event_ingestion.py::test_rejects_invalid_event_config_boundary must be materialized by Implementation Design as the explicit entrypoint for config and schema rejection.
explicitTestcases.implementationNotes: Do not replace this with unit-only validation. The acceptance must observe both API rejection and failure-record persistence.

explicitTestcases.name: sec-event-ingestion-v1-preserve-undefined-payload-fields
explicitTestcases.controlPoint: Submit a legal event whose payload includes all required configured fields plus at least one field not declared by the schema configuration.
explicitTestcases.observationPoint: The event is saved successfully, configured list columns exclude the undefined field, detail shows configured fields by label, and the undefined payload field appears only in the undefined-fields detail area and raw payload view.
explicitTestcases.acceptanceBoundary: tests/integration/security/test_security_event_ingestion.py::test_preserves_undefined_payload_fields_in_detail_only must be materialized by Implementation Design as the explicit entrypoint for undefined payload handling.
explicitTestcases.implementationNotes: V1 allows undefined payload fields for traceability; rejecting them or treating them as primary list columns violates intent.

explicitTestcases.name: sec-event-ingestion-v1-enforce-idempotency
explicitTestcases.controlPoint: Submit the same normalized event twice, submit the same sourceSystem plus eventId with a different eventTypeId, schemaVersion, or payload field value, and submit the same eventId from a different sourceSystem.
explicitTestcases.observationPoint: Identical repeat returns success with duplicate=true and no second business event; conflicting same-key submissions are rejected and recorded as idempotency conflicts; different sourceSystem plus same eventId is accepted as a distinct event.
explicitTestcases.acceptanceBoundary: tests/integration/security/test_security_event_ingestion.py::test_enforces_source_event_id_idempotency must be materialized by Implementation Design as the explicit entrypoint for sourceSystem plus eventId idempotency.
explicitTestcases.implementationNotes: The canonical comparison must use normalized event content; sourceSystem is part of the key, so global eventId uniqueness is not required.

explicitTestcases.name: sec-event-ingestion-v1-render-web-list-and-detail
explicitTestcases.controlPoint: Seed multiple accepted events with different receivedAt, occurredAt, sourceSystem, eventTypeId, severity, configured list payload fields, undefined fields, and raw payloads; open the Web list, apply time/source/type/severity filters, and navigate to a detail URL.
explicitTestcases.observationPoint: The list defaults to receivedAt descending, shows receivedAt, occurredAt, sourceSystem, event type display name, severity, summary, eventId, and configured list payload fields; filters constrain visible rows; the detail URL reopens the same event and displays base facts, labeled structured payload, undefined fields, and read-only raw payload with large-payload protection.
explicitTestcases.acceptanceBoundary: tests/e2e/security_center/test_security_event_inbox.py::test_web_lists_filters_and_opens_event_detail must be materialized by Implementation Design as the explicit entrypoint for operator Web consumption.
explicitTestcases.implementationNotes: If the repository does not yet have a browser e2e harness, Implementation Design must either establish one or document a narrower executable Web/API observation entrypoint without dropping list ordering, filters, stable detail URL, or large-payload protection.

explicitTestcases.name: sec-event-ingestion-v1-bound-failure-records
explicitTestcases.controlPoint: Submit illegal events for disallowed source, disallowed type, schema failure, payload validation failure, idempotency conflict, simulated persistence error, and an oversized illegal payload.
explicitTestcases.observationPoint: Business event list remains clean for illegal submissions, failure records are queryable with received time, source field, event type field, failure reason, and bounded request summary; database write failure returns failure instead of success, and oversized illegal payload handling stores only a bounded summary or rejects safely without storage/page instability.
explicitTestcases.acceptanceBoundary: tests/integration/security/test_security_event_ingestion.py::test_records_failed_receptions_without_business_event_pollution must be materialized by Implementation Design as the explicit entrypoint for failed reception records.
explicitTestcases.implementationNotes: Retention duration is a V1 non-goal. Implementation should not invent a default retention SLA, but it must avoid unbounded storage of oversized invalid payloads.

openQuestions.question: Should V1 add request authentication or source credentials because sourceSystem is caller-provided?
openQuestions.recommendedAnswer: No. Keep authentication and requester identity management out of V1, but make sourceSystem allow-listing, enabled flags, event-type authorization, and schema validation mandatory.
openQuestions.reason: The PRD explicitly excludes request authentication and identity management. Adding auth would expand scope, while accepting arbitrary sourceSystem without config controls would let untrusted data pollute the event inbox.

openQuestions.question: Should undefined payload fields cause rejection?
openQuestions.recommendedAnswer: No. Save legal events with undefined payload fields, mark those fields as undefined, exclude them from list main columns, and show them only in detail and raw payload areas.
openQuestions.reason: The PRD chooses traceability and integration tolerance over strict rejection for extra fields, but prevents operators from mistaking them for configured schema fields.

openQuestions.question: Should Security Center return success before persistence and complete storage asynchronously?
openQuestions.recommendedAnswer: No. Legal event persistence must complete before success; database write failure must return failure.
openQuestions.reason: The PRD makes durable-before-success the core reliability boundary. Returning early would create accepted-but-missing events and break operator trust.

openQuestions.question: Should implementation-design reuse existing Security Center audit-chain tests for this PRD?
openQuestions.recommendedAnswer: No. Reuse deploy/api, deploy/web, and tests architecture boundaries where appropriate, but create separate explicit entrypoints for security event ingestion rather than mixing them with audit integrity, lease recovery, or high-risk tool guard scenarios.
openQuestions.reason: The existing explicit security tests verify audit-chain trust semantics. This PRD is a different business capability: event intake, validation, persistence, Web inbox, and failure reception traceability.

openQuestions.question: Should V1 define retention periods or historical schema display behavior?
openQuestions.recommendedAnswer: No. Preserve them as non-goals and avoid implementing hidden default promises.
openQuestions.reason: The PRD explicitly excludes both. Silent defaults would create product commitments not accepted in intent design.

notes: Implementation Design should update root and local architecture contracts so deploy/api owns event intake, config validation, durable event and failure-record storage, query APIs, and persistence-error semantics; deploy/web owns operator event inbox list/detail rendering; tests owns explicit event-ingestion entrypoints and any supporting harness.

Implementation Design must decide the physical configuration artifact path and schema for source systems, event types, schema versions, payload fields, labels, enum values, maximum lengths, and list-display flags. The configuration file defines event contracts and display metadata only, not workflow rules.

Implementation Design must produce design/KG/ImplementationToCodingHandoff.json and design/KG/test-failure-records.json after materializing executable entrypoints. Expected initial failures are acceptable if production behavior is not yet implemented, but placeholder passing tests are not acceptable.

Do not edit production implementation in the intent stage. Do not add alerting, ticketing, notification, remediation, assignment, comments, exports, dashboards, Web self-service configuration editing, authentication, third-party external intake, retention promises, or historical schema display policy unless the intent graph is explicitly redesigned.
